summary: 9b continues the aggadic examination of Temple destruction. The bizayon kodashim (desecration of offerings) by Eli's sons is detailed (taking raw meat before burning fat). Then the First Temple's three cardinal sins are enumerated (avodah zarah, giluy arayot, shefichut damim) with biblical prooftexts. The Second Temple's sin - sinat chinam (baseless hatred) - is declared equivalent to all three combined, explaining why its exile was longer. The passage then moves to a remarkable contrast: former generations whose sins were public had a defined end to their exile; latter generations whose sins are hidden have no revealed end. R. Yochanan and R. Shimon debate which generation had greater merit. Finally, Reish Lakish's exchange with Rabba bar bar Chana in the Jordan River illustrates the failure of Babylonian Jewry to return to Israel in the era of Ezra: if they had all returned then (like a wall), the Temple would have been restored with the Shechina's full presence; because they came only partially (like a door), the Shechina remained diminished (like cedar, which is hollowed by rot). The daf closes on Rav Pappa's identification of who was actually in the Jordan River.

display.title: Eli's sons: delayed bird-offerings and desecration of sacrifices
display.oneLine: The two sins of Eli's sons are detailed: delaying women's bird-offerings (treated by scripture as sexual immorality) and taking raw meat before the fat was burned (degradation of sacred offerings).
display.shortSummary: Eli's sons' delay of women's bird-offerings: although they did not commit adultery, their delay kept women at the Tabernacle and thus separated from their husbands - scripture holds them accountable as if they had cohabited with the women. Their second sin: bizayon kodashim. Before the fat of an offering was burned on the altar, a servant of the priests would come and demand raw meat for roasting. The man bringing the sacrifice would protest: 'Let them first burn the fat today, then take whatever you wish.' The servant would say: 'No, give it now or I will take it by force.' This brazen taking of raw, unburned meat was the desecration.
display.whats: This section closes the account of Shiloh's sins. Both sins are connected: the delay of bird-offerings kept women from purity and their husbands; the raw-meat taking showed contempt for the sacrificial order. Both involve the priests treating Temple service as an opportunity for personal benefit rather than divine service.
display.hint: The raw-meat verse says they took 'before the fat was burned' - but more dramatically, before even the regular burning happened. The transgression is about sequence and disrespect, not just the taking of meat.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does scripture treat the delay of bird-offerings as equivalent to adultery?
learning.coreTension: The Talmud wants to be intellectually honest: Eli's sons were not adulterers. But the verse seems to say they were. The resolution: their sin had the effect that adultery would have had - women were kept from their husbands. Scripture 'credits' the transgressor with the downstream harm of his action.
learning.coreMove: This is a principle of scriptural attribution: when someone's action causes another person to commit (or be forced into a situation resembling) a sin, scripture attributes the original sin to the first person. The priests caused the women's extended absence from their husbands; scripture holds the priests responsible as if they had committed the act.
learning.resolution: The two sins of Shiloh are established: delayed bird-offerings (treated as giluy arayot) and taking raw meat prematurely (bizayon kodashim). Both involve priests prioritizing their own interests over proper Temple service.

takeaway.text: Scripture sometimes attributes a sin to a person based on the harm their action caused, even if they did not literally commit that sin. This principle - that indirect harm is treated as if direct - is used here to preserve Eli's sons from the charge of literal adultery while still holding them fully accountable.

reasoningPattern.notes: The Talmud regularly uses the principle that 'scripture ascribes the sin' to those who cause others to sin or suffer. Eli's sons caused women to remain impure and separated from their husbands - scripture treats this as their sin.

learning.ahaMoment: The raw-meat scene is vivid: a worshipper brings an animal, the priest's servant demands raw meat, the worshipper protests, the servant says 'give it now or I take it by force.' This is Temple service turned extortion - a shocking portrait of institutional corruption.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the 'delay of bird-offerings' means the priests refused to accept the offerings at all. In fact, they accepted them - but processed them slowly, keeping the women waiting for days.
learning.memoryAnchor: Two Eli-sons sins: delay the kanim (bird-offerings) - women stuck waiting, kept from husbands, scripture calls it immorality. Take raw meat before fat burned - intimidation and extortion of worshippers. Both: priests serving themselves, not God.

argumentFlow.label: Delayed bird-offerings kept women impure; scripture attributes the sin
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Eli's sons delayed the processing of women's bird-offerings. Since the women had to wait, they could not achieve purity and could not return to their husbands. Scripture treats the priests as if they had committed the act of cohabitation themselves.

sourceRefs.note: Delayed bird-offerings attributed as giluy arayot

argumentFlow.label: Bizayon kodashim: raw meat seized before fat was burned
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The desecration: a servant of the priests would demand raw meat before the fat was burned on the altar. The worshipper would protest. The servant would say: 'Give it now or I will take it by force.' This brazen seizing of raw meat - before the proper sacrificial sequence - was the bizayon kodashim.

concepts.definition: Bird-offerings (pairs of doves or pigeons) brought by women after childbirth, bodily flux, or other situations requiring purification. Delay in processing them prevented women from achieving purity.

visualizableElements.label: Raw meat seizure scene
visualizableElements.description: A worshipper bringing an animal to the altar; a priest's servant aggressively demanding raw, uncooked meat before the fat is burned; the worshipper protesting; the servant threatening force. Captures the extortion aspect of bizayon kodashim.

quizSeeds.question: Why does scripture treat the delay of women's bird-offerings as equivalent to sexual immorality?
quizSeeds.answer: Because the delay kept the women at the Tabernacle waiting, preventing them from achieving purity and returning to their husbands. Scripture attributes the downstream harm (forced separation from husbands) to the priests who caused it.

quizSeeds.question: What was the specific act of bizayon kodashim by Eli's sons?
quizSeeds.answer: Their servants would demand raw meat before the fat of the offering was burned on the altar. If the worshipper refused, they would take the meat by force. This violated the sacrificial order and treated sacred meat as personal property.

misconceptions.misconception: Eli's sons refused to accept women's bird-offerings entirely.
misconceptions.correction: They accepted the offerings but processed them slowly, causing women to wait extended periods - preventing them from achieving purity and returning to their husbands.

relatedSugyot.reason: 9a introduced the Shiloh destruction and began the Eli's sons rehabilitation
Immediately following: the First Temple's three cardinal sins and Second Temple's sinat chinam

requiresUnderstanding: The distinction between literal and attributed sin in Talmudic reading

display.title: First Temple: three cardinal sins. Second Temple: sinat chinam equals all three. Why longer exile?
display.oneLine: The First Temple was destroyed for avodah zarah, giluy arayot, and shefichut damim (three cardinal sins); the Second Temple - where Torah was studied and kindness practiced - was destroyed for sinat chinam (baseless hatred), which scripture weights as equivalent to all three combined; the paradox of good people in a long exile is addressed.
display.shortSummary: A Tosefta: the First Temple was destroyed for three things: avodah zarah (idolatry), giluy arayot (sexual immorality), and shefichut damim (bloodshed). Each is supported by a biblical verse. Idolatry: 'the bed is too short for both to lie' (a bed made for one - either God or the idol). Sexual immorality: 'the daughters of Zion walked with outstretched necks' (detailed elaboration of provocative dress and behavior). Bloodshed: Manasseh filled Jerusalem with innocent blood. The Second Temple: those generations studied Torah, performed mitzvot, and practiced kindness. Why did the Temple fall? Because of sinat chinam - baseless hatred. This teaches that sinat chinam is weighed as equal to the three cardinal sins combined, and therefore the exile from the Second Temple was longer (no revealed date of return). The paradox: the First Temple generation's sins were open, so the end of exile was revealed (70 years). The Second Temple generation's sins were hidden, so the end of exile has not been revealed.
display.whats: This is the most famous passage in tractate Yoma. The sin of sinat chinam being equivalent to the three cardinal sins has shaped Jewish self-understanding for two millennia. The counterintuitive claim - that a generation of Torah scholars fell because of interpersonal hatred - challenges any simple equation of religious observance with communal virtue.
display.hint: The First Temple people openly worshipped idols but their prophets could rebuke them and the exile had an end-date. The Second Temple people were righteous by external measures but harbored hidden hatred - no prophet to rebuke hidden sin, no revealed end-date.

learning.learnerQuestion: How can sinat chinam be equal to all three cardinal sins combined? Isn't baseless hatred much less severe than murder or idolatry?
learning.coreTension: The three cardinal sins are the ones for which a Jew must die rather than violate (yehareg v'al ya'avor). Sinat chinam is not in that category. So how does the Talmud place them on equal footing? The answer is social-structural: the three cardinal sins destroy individuals (murder) or relationships with God (idolatry) or family purity (immorality). Sinat chinam destroys the entire social fabric - every relationship, every community. A society where sinat chinam is pervasive is ungovernable, unjust, and ultimately unsurvivable.
learning.coreMove: The comparison is not about individual severity but about communal destruction capacity. One act of murder kills one person; pervasive sinat chinam prevents communal coherence, justice, and the social conditions that make Jewish life possible. The Temple requires a united people - sinat chinam makes that unity impossible.
learning.resolution: The passage implies a responsibility: because sinat chinam's end is not revealed (unlike the First Exile's 70 years), the corrective lies in human action - repairing interpersonal relationships - not in waiting for a prophetic announcement.

takeaway.text: Individual piety is not sufficient for communal survival. A generation of Torah scholars who hate each other cannot maintain a Temple. The Talmud is not saying hatred is worse than murder - it is saying that pervasive hatred in an otherwise righteous community is uniquely destructive because it has no external corrective (no prophet can rebuke what is hidden).

reasoningPattern.notes: The Talmud distinguishes between sins that are correctable through prophecy/rebuke and sins that are hidden and structural. Sinat chinam is hidden and structural - therefore no prophetic corrective, no revealed endpoint.

learning.ahaMoment: The First Temple generation's sins were worse individually but their exile was shorter. The Second Temple generation's sins were 'lesser' individually but their exile was longer. The Talmud explains: the First generation's sins were open (prophets could rebuke them); the Second generation's hidden hatred had no external corrective. This inverts our intuition about which exile should have been worse.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the First Temple people were openly sinful because prophets 'failed' to stop them. The Talmud implies the opposite: because the prophets could identify and rebuke the sin, the generation could eventually repent, setting an endpoint. Hidden sinat chinam cannot be rebuked because it does not appear as sin.
learning.memoryAnchor: First Temple = three open sins (idolatry, immorality, bloodshed) - open sins, prophets rebuke, exile has end. Second Temple = sinat chinam (hidden) - no prophetic rebuke, no revealed end. Lesson: righteous individuals in a hateful community cannot maintain a Temple.

argumentFlow.label: First Temple destroyed for three cardinal sins
argumentFlow.speaker: Tosefta
argumentFlow.text: The First Temple was destroyed for three sins practiced in it: avodah zarah, giluy arayot, and shefichut damim.

sourceRefs.note: Three cardinal sins of First Temple era

argumentFlow.label: Idolatry: the 'narrow bed' metaphor
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yonatan
argumentFlow.text: 'The bed is too short for both to stretch out' (Isaiah) - R. Yonatan: this bed [= the Sanctuary/Holy of Holies] is too narrow to hold two [= God and the idol]. The idol of Manasseh in the Temple is the referent.

sourceRefs.note: Isaiah verse interpreted as idolatry in the Temple

argumentFlow.label: Sexual immorality: R. Yitzchak on daughters of Zion
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yitzchak
argumentFlow.text: R. Yitzchak expounds the details: they would bring musk and sprinkle it in their sandals, walking near young men of Israel to arouse desire. Isaiah's description of 'outstretched necks, winking eyes, mincing steps, anklet-jingling' is a catalogue of deliberate provocation.

sourceRefs.note: R. Yitzchak's elaboration on daughters of Zion

argumentFlow.label: Sexual immorality verse: daughters of Zion walking with outstretched necks
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Isaiah's description of the daughters of Zion - walking with outstretched necks, winking eyes, mincing steps, anklet-jingling - is the biblical basis for the giluy arayot charge against the First Temple generation.

sourceRefs.note: Isaiah verse interpreted as sexual immorality

argumentFlow.label: Bloodshed: Manasseh filled Jerusalem with innocent blood
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The verse in II Kings: 'And Manasseh also shed very much innocent blood until he filled Jerusalem from end to end.'

concepts.definition: Baseless hatred - hatred without legitimate cause. The Talmud identifies this as the reason for the destruction of the Second Temple and the cause of the longer exile.
One should be killed rather than transgress - the three cardinal sins (avodah zarah, giluy arayot, shefichut damim) are in this category: a Jew must die rather than violate them.
Baseless love - the corrective to sinat chinam, often mentioned as the key to the redemption. Not explicitly in this passage but implied as the repair.

visualizableElements.label: First vs. Second Temple sin profiles
visualizableElements.description: Two columns: First Temple (idolatry, immorality, bloodshed - open, visible, rebukable) vs. Second Temple (Torah observance, kindness, mitzvot - outwardly righteous but internally hateful). The contrast is the lesson: external piety is insufficient.

visualizableElements.label: Sinat chinam vs. three cardinal sins
visualizableElements.description: A scale showing sinat chinam on one side balanced against avodah zarah + giluy arayot + shefichut damim on the other. The Talmud declares them equal. Visual helps learners grasp the counterintuitive magnitude.

quizSeeds.question: What were the three sins for which the First Temple was destroyed?
quizSeeds.answer: Avodah zarah (idolatry), giluy arayot (sexual immorality), and shefichut damim (bloodshed).

quizSeeds.question: Why is the exile after the Second Temple's destruction longer than the exile after the First Temple's?
quizSeeds.answer: The First generation's sins were revealed (open) - prophets could rebuke them and the exile had a revealed end (70 years). The Second generation's sin (sinat chinam) was hidden - no external rebuke possible, no revealed end to the exile.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Talmud explain that the Second Temple generation - who studied Torah and performed kindness - was destroyed?
quizSeeds.answer: Because sinat chinam (baseless hatred) is considered equivalent in destructive power to all three cardinal sins combined. External piety without internal unity cannot sustain a Temple.

misconceptions.misconception: The Second Temple generation was hypocritical - they didn't really practice Torah and mitzvot.
misconceptions.correction: The Talmud affirms they genuinely studied Torah, performed mitzvot, and practiced kindness. Their failure was not external behavior but internal: hatred of each other that was invisible to prophetic rebuke.

misconceptions.misconception: Sinat chinam is worse than murder or idolatry in terms of individual severity.
misconceptions.correction: The comparison is about communal destructive capacity, not individual severity. Sinat chinam destroys the social fabric of the entire community, making communal Jewish life impossible even among otherwise observant individuals.

relatedSugyot.reason: Eli-sons sins that preceded this broader Temple-destruction discussion
Immediately following: why the Second Temple exile is longer than the First

requiresUnderstanding: The three cardinal sins (avodah zarah, giluy arayot, shefichut damim) and their 'yehareg v'al ya'avor' status
What sinat chinam means - hatred without justification

display.title: Second Temple destroyed for sinat chinam; hidden sin means longer exile
display.oneLine: The Second Temple generation studied Torah and practiced kindness, yet was destroyed for sinat chinam - declared equal to all three cardinal sins combined - and because their sin was hidden, their exile has no revealed end.
display.shortSummary: The Second Temple generation engaged in Torah study, mitzvot, and acts of kindness. Why was the Temple destroyed? For sinat chinam (baseless hatred). This teaches that sinat chinam equals the three cardinal sins combined. Furthermore: the First Temple generation's sins were revealed (prophets could rebuke them) - their exile had a revealed end (70 years). The Second Temple generation's sins were hidden (sinat chinam does not appear as sin from the outside) - their exile has no revealed end. The lesson: hidden sin is more corrosive to communal life than open sin, because it cannot be identified, rebuked, or corrected.
display.whats: This is the most famous passage in tractate Yoma. The sin of sinat chinam being equivalent to the three cardinal sins is not a claim about individual severity but about communal destruction capacity. The Temple requires a united people; sinat chinam makes unity impossible even among otherwise observant individuals.
display.hint: The First Temple people openly sinned but their exile had an end-date. The Second Temple people appeared righteous but harbored hidden hatred with no prophetic corrective and no revealed end. Hidden sin is uniquely dangerous.

learning.learnerQuestion: How can sinat chinam be equal to all three cardinal sins combined?
learning.coreTension: The three cardinal sins (avodah zarah, giluy arayot, shefichut damim) are those for which a Jew must die rather than violate them. Sinat chinam is not in that category. So how does the Talmud equate them? The answer is structural: those sins destroy individuals or relationships with God. Sinat chinam destroys the entire social fabric - every relationship, every community. The Temple requires a united people.
learning.coreMove: The comparison is communal destruction capacity, not individual severity. Pervasive sinat chinam prevents communal coherence, justice, and the social conditions that make Jewish life possible. One act of murder kills one person; pervasive hatred prevents the community from functioning at all.
learning.resolution: Because sinat chinam's end is not revealed, the corrective lies in human action - repairing interpersonal relationships - not in waiting for prophetic announcement. The Talmud implies personal responsibility for the ongoing exile.

takeaway.text: Individual piety is not sufficient for communal survival. A generation of Torah scholars who hate each other cannot maintain a Temple. And because the sin is hidden, there is no external corrective - only self-correction.

reasoningPattern.notes: Sinat chinam is hidden and structural - no prophetic rebuke possible, no revealed endpoint. The distinction between correctable-open-sin and uncorrectable-hidden-sin is the key analytical move.

learning.ahaMoment: The First Temple generation's sins were worse individually but their exile was shorter. The Second Temple generation's sins were 'lesser' individually but their exile is longer. Reason: the First generation could be rebuked (sins were open); the Second generation cannot be rebuked (sinat chinam looks like nothing from the outside). This inverts our intuition.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the First Temple people were openly sinful because the prophets 'failed' to stop them. The Talmud implies the opposite: because prophets could identify the sin, the generation could eventually repent, setting an endpoint for exile.
learning.memoryAnchor: First Temple = three open sins + prophets who rebuke + 70-year exile. Second Temple = sinat chinam (hidden) + no prophetic rebuke + no revealed exile end. Open sin has a cure; hidden sin does not.

argumentFlow.label: Second Temple: Torah and kindness practiced - yet destroyed for sinat chinam
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Second Temple generation engaged in Torah study, mitzvot, and acts of kindness. Why was the Temple destroyed? Because of sinat chinam (baseless hatred). This teaches that sinat chinam is weighed equally against the three cardinal sins combined.

sourceRefs.note: Second Temple destroyed for sinat chinam

argumentFlow.label: Why Second Temple exile is longer: hidden sin vs. revealed sin
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The First Temple generation's sins were revealed - their exile was given a revealed end (70 years in Babylon). The Second Temple generation's sins were hidden (sinat chinam does not appear as sin from the outside) - their exile has no revealed end.

sourceRefs.note: Hidden vs. revealed sin explains exile duration

concepts.definition: Baseless hatred - hatred without cause. The Talmud identifies it as the cause of the Second Temple's destruction and the reason the exile has no revealed end.

visualizableElements.label: First vs. Second Temple sin profiles
visualizableElements.description: Two columns: First Temple (idolatry, immorality, bloodshed - open, visible, rebukable, 70-year exile) vs. Second Temple (Torah, mitzvot, kindness externally - but internal sinat chinam, no revealed exile end). The contrast is the lesson.

visualizableElements.label: Sinat chinam vs. three cardinal sins on a scale
visualizableElements.description: A balance scale: sinat chinam on one side, avodah zarah + giluy arayot + shefichut damim on the other. The Talmud declares them equal in communal destructive capacity.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Talmud explain that the Second Temple generation - who studied Torah and performed kindness - was destroyed?
quizSeeds.answer: Because sinat chinam (baseless hatred) is considered equivalent in destructive power to all three cardinal sins combined. External piety without internal unity cannot sustain a Temple.

quizSeeds.question: Why is the exile after the Second Temple's destruction longer than after the First Temple?
quizSeeds.answer: The First generation's sins were revealed (open) - prophets could rebuke them and the exile had a revealed end (70 years). The Second generation's sin (sinat chinam) was hidden - no external rebuke possible, no revealed end.

misconceptions.misconception: Sinat chinam is worse than murder or idolatry in terms of individual severity.
misconceptions.correction: The comparison is about communal destructive capacity, not individual severity. Sinat chinam destroys the social fabric of the entire community, making communal Jewish life impossible even among otherwise observant individuals.

relatedSugyot.reason: The First Temple three cardinal sins that this passage contrasts with sinat chinam
Immediately following: First Temple sinat chinam was limited to nesi'im; former vs. latter generation debate

requiresUnderstanding: The First Temple / Second Temple comparison from 9b-s02
Why hidden sin is theologically more problematic than open sin

display.title: First Temple sinat chinam limited to nesi'im; R. Yochanan vs. R. Shimon on generational merit
display.oneLine: The sinat chinam in the First Temple era was only among the princes (nesi'im), so the common people could be rebuked and the exile had an end; R. Yochanan and R. Shimon disagree on which generation had greater merit.
display.shortSummary: But wait - was the First Temple free of sinat chinam entirely? No: a verse ('cry out... for the nesi'im of Israel') shows that sinat chinam existed in the First Temple period - but it was confined to the nesi'im (princes and leaders). Because only the leaders sinned with sinat chinam, the common people could still be reached by prophetic rebuke, and the exile could end. R. Yochanan: former generations (First Temple era) were greater, as proven by the Temple being restored to them. R. Shimon ben Lakish: latter generations are greater, because they maintained Torah study and mitzvot despite exile and oppression - a greater achievement.
display.whats: This section refines the sinat chinam argument: it was not entirely absent from the First Temple era. The structural key is who sinned: only the leadership. When leadership sins and the community can still be rebuked, the system can correct. When the community itself sins with hidden hatred, no correction is possible.
display.hint: The debate between R. Yochanan and R. Shimon about generational merit is not just historical curiosity - it is a live question about whether the exile proves God's displeasure (R. Yochanan: former were better because Temple was given to them) or resilience under oppression proves current greatness (R. Shimon: latter are better).

learning.learnerQuestion: If the First Temple generation had sinat chinam too, what makes their exile shorter?
learning.coreTension: The sinat chinam argument seems to break down: both Temple eras had sinat chinam. The resolution is scope: First Temple sinat chinam was among the leadership only. This allowed prophets to rebuke the people (who had not internalized the hatred) and set an endpoint for exile. Second Temple sinat chinam was pervasive across the community, making it invisible and unreachable by prophecy.
learning.coreMove: Leadership sin is correctable; communal sin is not. When only the top sinners, the bottom can be rebuked and saved. When the whole community carries hidden hatred, there is no external vantage point from which to identify or rebuke it.
learning.resolution: R. Yochanan and R. Shimon's debate remains unresolved in the text. Both positions have internal logic. The Talmud preserves the disagreement without adjudicating it - an implicit acknowledgment that the question of generational merit is genuinely contested.

takeaway.text: Leadership corruption that stays at the top is correctable. When corruption trickles into the social fabric of ordinary relationships (as sinat chinam does), it becomes invisible and systemic - and therefore uncheckable.

reasoningPattern.notes: A recurring Talmudic analysis: the same sin at different social levels has different consequences. Leadership-level sinat chinam is rebukeable; community-level sinat chinam is not.

learning.ahaMoment: R. Yochanan's 'proof' that former generations are greater (the Temple was given to them) is itself challenged by R. Shimon's counter: maintaining Torah under oppression requires MORE merit, not less. The argument is not resolved - the Talmud honors both views.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may think the First Temple was 'better' because its era had fewer High Priests. The statistics (18 vs. 300+) are about institutional stability, not individual righteousness. The generational merit debate is a separate question.
learning.memoryAnchor: First Temple sinat chinam = leadership only = prophets could still reach the people = exile had an end. R. Yochanan: former greater (Temple given to them). Reish Lakish: latter greater (Torah despite oppression). No resolution.

argumentFlow.label: First Temple's sinat chinam limited to the nesi'im
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A verse ('Cry out... for the nesi'im of Israel') shows that the sinat chinam in the First Temple period was confined to the princes and leaders. The common people could still be rebuked by prophets. This is why the First Temple exile had a revealed end.

sourceRefs.note: First Temple sinat chinam limited to leaders - verse from nesi'im

argumentFlow.label: R. Yochanan and Reish Lakish: which generation had greater merit?
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan and Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: R. Yochanan: former generations are greater - their revealed sins/exile prove they could be rebuked and return; and the Temple was restored to them. Reish Lakish: latter generations are greater - they maintain Torah study and mitzvot despite exile and oppression, an even more impressive achievement.

sourceRefs.note: Dispute about relative greatness of former vs. latter generations

concepts.definition: Princes or leaders of Israel. In this context: the First Temple leaders who harbored sinat chinam among themselves while the people could still be rebuked.

visualizableElements.label: Leadership-level vs. communal-level sinat chinam
visualizableElements.description: Two diagrams: First Temple (sin confined to top leadership tier, people below still reachable by prophets) vs. Second Temple (sinat chinam diffused throughout community, no vantage point for rebuke). Same sin, different scope = different outcomes.

quizSeeds.question: Why did the First Temple's sinat chinam not prevent exile from ending, when the Second Temple's did?
quizSeeds.answer: The First Temple's sinat chinam was confined to the nesi'im (leaders). The common people could be rebuked by prophets and the exile could end. The Second Temple's sinat chinam was community-wide and hidden, beyond prophetic rebuke.

quizSeeds.question: What are R. Yochanan's and Reish Lakish's respective views on which generation had greater merit?
quizSeeds.answer: R. Yochanan: former generations were greater (the Temple was given to them as proof). Reish Lakish: latter generations are greater (they maintain Torah and mitzvot despite the additional burden of exile and oppression).

misconceptions.misconception: Both Temple eras were equally affected by sinat chinam.
misconceptions.correction: The First Temple era had sinat chinam only among the nesi'im (leadership), allowing prophets to rebuke the people. The Second Temple era had pervasive, community-wide sinat chinam, leaving no external corrective.

relatedSugyot.reason: The sinat chinam teaching that this section refines and nuances
Immediately following: Reish Lakish's rebuke of Babylonian Jews in the Jordan

requiresUnderstanding: The nesi'im as a separate category within the First Temple community

display.title: Reish Lakish in the Jordan rebukes Babylonian Jews who did not return with Ezra
display.oneLine: Reish Lakish, swimming in the Jordan, denounces Babylonian Jewry for their failure to return en masse with Ezra - had they all come like a wall, the Shechina would have returned fully (silver); since they came partially like a door, it returned only partially (cedar with rot).
display.shortSummary: Reish Lakish was swimming in the Jordan River. Rabba bar bar Chana extended a hand to help him. Reish Lakish said: 'By God, I despise you Babylonians!' He quoted Song of Songs 8:9: 'If she is a wall, we will build on her a tower of silver; if she is a door, we will panel her with cedar boards.' Applied: if all Babylonian Jews had returned to Israel together with Ezra (like a wall - unified, solid), the Shechina would have returned fully and the Second Temple would have been like silver (imperishable). Since they came only partially (like a door - swinging, incomplete), the Shechina returned only partially, and the Temple was like cedar that harbors rotting sasmagor within.
display.whats: This aggadic passage connects two themes: the communal failure to return with Ezra and the diminished spiritual presence in the Second Temple. The wall/door metaphor from Song of Songs is applied as a precise allegory. Reish Lakish's rebuke is historical, not personal - he is criticizing the Babylonian generation of Ezra's era, not the sage before him.
display.hint: Reish Lakish is not angry at Rabba bar bar Chana personally - he did make aliyah. The rebuke is directed at the historical generation that stayed in Babylon when Ezra called them. The Jordan River scene is the dramatic vehicle for a retrospective critique.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why is Reish Lakish so angry at the Babylonian Jews if many of them eventually returned?
learning.coreTension: The moment matters: Ezra's era was the appointed time for return. Had all Jews returned then, the Shechina would have returned fully to the Second Temple. Because most stayed in Babylon (comfortable, prosperous), the Temple was built without the full divine presence. Those who eventually returned (like Rabba bar bar Chana) still represent the community that missed the moment.
learning.coreMove: Wall = unified complete return = silver (imperishable, full Shechina). Door = partial return = cedar (some solid wood, some rot = partial Shechina). The metaphor is from Song of Songs but applied as a historical-theological judgment about the Second Temple's spiritual state.
learning.resolution: The cedar metaphor is explained further in s06: sasmagor (a wood-boring rot) is what eats cedar from within. It represents the spiritual diminishment caused by the incomplete aliyah. The bat kol that replaced prophecy is part of the same diminishment.

takeaway.text: Historical moments of communal redemption are not infinitely extensible. The window for Ezra's aliyah was specific. Missing it had consequences that persist: not punishment but spiritual diminishment - a partial Shechina instead of a full one.

reasoningPattern.notes: This passage pairs with sinat chinam as another form of communal failure. Sinat chinam destroyed from within; failure to make aliyah with Ezra diminished the Second Temple's spiritual foundation from the start.

learning.ahaMoment: The Song of Songs verse is usually read as romantic. Reish Lakish reads it as a national allegory: 'she' is the Jewish people; 'wall' is communal solidarity and complete return; 'silver' is the imperishable divine presence. The verse encodes a conditional promise that the generation of Ezra failed to fulfill.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may miss that Reish Lakish is not condemning Rabba bar bar Chana personally. He is using the moment of the outstretched hand (Babylonian Jew helping him) to make a historical-communal point about Babylonian Jewry's collective missed opportunity.
learning.memoryAnchor: Wall + silver = all return, Shechina complete. Door + cedar = partial return, Shechina partial (sasmagor rot within). Reish Lakish in the Jordan = dramatic stage for a critique of Babylonian Jewry's failure to return en masse with Ezra.

argumentFlow.label: Reish Lakish in the Jordan denounces Babylonian Jewry
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: Reish Lakish was swimming in the Jordan River. Rabba bar bar Chana extended a hand. Reish Lakish said: 'By God, I despise you Babylonians!' He quoted Song of Songs 8:9: if all of you had come up like a wall (together, in Ezra's time), you would be like silver (imperishable Shechina). Since you came up like a door (partially), you are like cedar (which harbors sasmagor rot within).

sourceRefs.note: Reish Lakish's rebuke of Babylonian non-aliyah

argumentFlow.label: Why silver vs. cedar? Wall vs. door explained
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Silver does not decay. If all Babylonian Jews had returned together with Ezra, the Shechina would have returned fully and the Second Temple would have had the permanence of silver. Cedar, by contrast, harbors sasmagor - a wood-boring rot that destroys it from within. Partial return = partial Shechina = cedar-like diminishment.

sourceRefs.note: Wall/door metaphor explained: silver vs. cedar

argumentFlow.label: Gemara asks: would Reish Lakish speak with Rabba bar bar Chana?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara wonders: given Reish Lakish's strict selectiveness about with whom he associated, would he really have been in conversation with Rabba bar bar Chana?

sourceRefs.note: Question about whether Reish Lakish would speak with Rabba bar bar Chana

concepts.definition: A wood-boring rot or insect that destroys cedar from within. Used metaphorically for the partial Shechina presence in the Second Temple due to incomplete aliyah.

visualizableElements.label: Wall vs. door: two visions of aliyah
visualizableElements.description: Left panel: solid stone wall = all Jews returning together with Ezra = silver tower built on top (imperishable Shechina). Right panel: wooden door = partial return = cedar paneling (sasmagor rot visible inside). The Song of Songs verse made visual.

visualizableElements.label: Reish Lakish and Rabba bar bar Chana in the Jordan
visualizableElements.description: Two sages in the Jordan River - one swimming, one extending a hand. The moment of historical rebuke: 'I despise you Babylonians.' The Jordan River itself is symbolic - the boundary of the Land of Israel.

quizSeeds.question: What is the wall-and-door metaphor from Song of Songs and how does Reish Lakish apply it to Babylonian Jewry?
quizSeeds.answer: Song of Songs: 'If she is a wall, we will build on her silver; if she is a door, we will panel her with cedar.' If all Babylonian Jews had returned with Ezra (wall = unified, complete), the Shechina would have returned fully (silver = imperishable). Since only some returned (door = partial, incomplete), the Shechina returned only partially (cedar = harbors rot).

quizSeeds.question: Why is Reish Lakish's rebuke not directed at Rabba bar bar Chana personally?
quizSeeds.answer: Rabba bar bar Chana did make aliyah to Israel. Reish Lakish is criticizing the Babylonian Jewish community as a whole for not returning with Ezra when the moment was right. The Jordan River scene is the dramatic vehicle for a historical-communal critique.

misconceptions.misconception: Reish Lakish is saying the Babylonian Jews who stayed behind sinned grievously.
misconceptions.correction: The critique is about a missed historical opportunity, not a grave sin. Those who stayed in Babylon were comfortable and prosperous - they had no obvious reason to leave. But their comfort cost the Second Temple its full divine presence.

relatedSugyot.reason: The former vs. latter generations debate that precedes this Reish Lakish passage
Immediately following: sasmagor explained, bat kol introduced, Rav Pappa's clarification

requiresUnderstanding: The Babylonian exile context and Ezra's return to Israel
Song of Songs as a text that allows allegorical interpretation

display.title: Sasmagor explained; bat kol replaces prophecy; Rav Pappa clarifies who was in the Jordan
display.oneLine: Ulla explains sasmagor (the rot that devours cedar); R. Abba states that after the last prophets died, only the bat kol remained; Rav Pappa resolves the uncertainty about whether it was Reish Lakish or Rabba bar bar Chana who was swimming in the Jordan.
display.shortSummary: What is sasmagor (the cedar-destroying rot mentioned by Reish Lakish)? Ulla explains: it is a boring creature that destroys cedar from within, eroding and hollowing its core. R. Abba then states: from when Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi (the last prophets) died, the Ruach HaKodesh (prophetic spirit) ceased from Israel. Yet they still had the bat kol (heavenly voice) as a residual form of divine communication. Rav Pappa resolves the uncertainty: it was either Reish Lakish swimming with Zeira extending a hand, or Rabba bar bar Chana swimming with R. Elazar extending a hand. The identities are uncertain; the rebuke itself is the lesson.
display.whats: This closing passage on 9b ties together the cedar/sasmagor metaphor with the bat kol theme: both are statements about spiritual diminishment. Prophecy ended with the last prophets; divine communication was reduced to the bat kol. This mirrors the Second Temple's diminished Shechina. The daf ends with ambiguity (who was in the Jordan?) as an acknowledgment that historical memory sometimes preserves the lesson without preserving every detail.
display.hint: Sasmagor as the explanation for cedar's vulnerability parallels the sinat chinam as the explanation for the Second Temple's vulnerability. Both are hidden internal forces of destruction.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the passage about Reish Lakish end with an unresolved question about who was actually in the Jordan?
learning.coreTension: The Talmud often preserves authentic uncertainty. Rav Pappa does not know which pair of sages was in the Jordan - he offers two possibilities. This is not a sign of editorial sloppiness but of historical honesty: the lesson (Babylonian Jewry failed to return en masse) is what matters; the specific participants are secondary.
learning.coreMove: Bat kol as a closing theme: after full prophecy ended, Israel still had the bat kol as a residual channel. The same diminishment that gave the Second Temple less Shechina than the First also gave the Second Temple era less prophecy than the First Temple era. The bat kol is the 'cedar version' of divine communication - partly intact, partly diminished.
learning.resolution: Rav Pappa's clarification ends the daf: either Reish Lakish/Zeira or Rabba bar bar Chana/R. Elazar were in the Jordan. The lesson stands regardless of the participants. The tractate continues on 10a.

takeaway.text: Divine communication, like the Shechina itself, underwent diminishment between the Temple eras. Full prophecy gave way to bat kol; full Shechina gave way to partial. Both are the spiritual consequence of communal failures - sinat chinam and incomplete aliyah.

reasoningPattern.notes: The 9b daf's final passage ties together three diminishments: cedar (partial return/partial Shechina), bat kol (partial prophecy), and uncertainty (partial historical memory). All three express the same theme.

learning.ahaMoment: The bat kol replaces prophecy - but the bat kol itself was used in this very discussion to signal divine ambiguity (who was in the Jordan). The Talmud is using the bat kol even as it explains that the bat kol is a lesser form of communication. The medium and the message are one.
learning.learningBlocker: Students may not connect the sasmagor/cedar explanation to the bat kol discussion. Both are about the same diminishment: just as cedar has internal rot despite outward integrity, Israel in the Second Temple era had divine presence but with internal absence.
learning.memoryAnchor: Sasmagor = cedar's internal rot = partial Shechina in Second Temple. Bat kol = residual divine communication after prophecy ended = partial contact. Rav Pappa = uncertainty about who was in the Jordan = partial historical memory. Three diminishments, one theme: the consequence of incomplete return.

argumentFlow.label: Ulla: sasmagor is the rot that devours cedar from within
argumentFlow.speaker: Ulla
argumentFlow.text: What is sasmagor? Ulla explains: it is a boring creature or rot that destroys cedar from within, eroding and hollowing its core while the exterior may appear intact.

argumentFlow.label: R. Abba: bat kol replaced prophecy after the last prophets died
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Abba
argumentFlow.text: After the last prophets (Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi) died, the Ruach HaKodesh (prophetic spirit) ceased from Israel. Yet they still had the bat kol (heavenly voice) as a residual form of divine communication.

sourceRefs.note: Bat kol replaces prophecy after last prophets

argumentFlow.label: Return to the Jordan scene: who was actually there?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara returns to the Jordan scene and raises a question: would Reish Lakish speak with Rabba bar bar Chana? Reish Lakish was strict about his associates.

sourceRefs.note: Question about who was in the Jordan

argumentFlow.label: Rav Pappa: two possibilities for who was in the Jordan
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Pappa
argumentFlow.text: Rav Pappa resolves: either it was Reish Lakish swimming with Zeira extending a hand, or Rabba bar bar Chana swimming with R. Elazar extending a hand. The rebuke was given by one of these pairs; the exact identities are uncertain.

sourceRefs.note: Rav Pappa's resolution of the Jordan scene uncertainty

argumentFlow.label: Closing: R. Yochanan confirms the rebuke was appropriate
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: R. Yochanan affirms that the rebuke ('I despise you Babylonians') was appropriate: not all of Babylonian Jewry ascended with Ezra, and as a result the Shechina did not dwell fully in the Second Temple. The daf ends with this historical affirmation.

sourceRefs.note: R. Yochanan confirms the Reish Lakish rebuke

concepts.definition: Heavenly voice - the form of divine communication that replaced direct prophecy after the last prophets (Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi). Lower in authority than prophecy but still used in halachic and narrative contexts.
The Holy Spirit or prophetic spirit - the direct channel of divine inspiration available to prophets. It ceased from Israel when the last prophets (Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi) died.

visualizableElements.label: Prophecy vs. bat kol: diminishment of divine communication
visualizableElements.description: Two levels: First Temple era (direct prophecy, full Shechina) vs. Second Temple era (bat kol only, partial Shechina). Mirrors the cedar/silver distinction and the partial/complete aliyah distinction. Three diminishments expressing one theme.

visualizableElements.label: The four possible Jordan participants
visualizableElements.description: Two alternative pairings per Rav Pappa: (A) Reish Lakish swimming + Zeira extending hand; (B) Rabba bar bar Chana swimming + R. Elazar extending hand. Shows how historical memory preserves the lesson but not always the precise participants.

quizSeeds.question: What replaced prophecy after Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi died?
quizSeeds.answer: The bat kol (heavenly voice) - a lower form of divine communication that could still provide guidance but lacked the direct authority of prophecy.

quizSeeds.question: Who does Rav Pappa say was in the Jordan River?
quizSeeds.answer: Rav Pappa says it was either Reish Lakish (swimming) with Zeira (extending hand), or Rabba bar bar Chana (swimming) with R. Elazar (extending hand). The exact pair is uncertain.

misconceptions.misconception: The bat kol was equally authoritative as direct prophecy.
misconceptions.correction: The bat kol was a step down from prophecy. The passage introduces it as a consolation - something of divine communication remained - but the full prophetic channel ended with the last three prophets.

relatedSugyot.reason: The Reish Lakish Jordan scene that this passage resolves and closes
The Second Temple corruption statistics that set the context for the 9b Temple-destruction theme

requiresUnderstanding: What bat kol is and how it differs from direct prophecy

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi on 'they delayed the bird-offerings of their wives': Eli's sons would wait for women who came to purify themselves after childbirth (or zavah status) to bring their bird-offerings. The women would wait at the Tabernacle and the sons would deliberately delay processing the offerings, preventing the women from returning home.

Rashi: 'the text treats it as if they had slept with them' - scripture's language 'and they lay with the women' is interpreted as: because the sons caused the women to remain at the Tabernacle, unable to return to their husbands, it is as if they had themselves cohabited with those women. Attribution by effect.

Rashi on 'min hastarer rei'a echad' (from the word rera = bad companion): the 'narrow bed' in Isaiah means the Temple, which is too small for two rivals - God and the idol of Manasseh. 'Two bad companions' = God and the idol placed side by side in the same space.

Rashi: 'rasheha b'shochad yishpotu' (its leaders judge for bribes) - this is the end of the verse in Micah that R. Yonatan quotes to show that the sinat chinam in the First Temple era was concentrated among the nesi'im (leaders), not the general population.

Rashi: 'aleihem' (upon them) - the verse refers to the First Temple; the three divine decrees (destruction of fields, Jerusalem, and Temple Mount) are directed at those who sinned. Confirms the First Temple context.

Rashi: 'three decrees: field, ruins, forest heights' - three parts of the verse in Micah ('therefore Zion will be plowed as a field, Jerusalem will become ruins, the Temple Mount will become forest heights') correspond to the three cardinal sins.

Rashi: 'whose sin was revealed - their end was revealed' - since the First Temple generation's sins were open (prophets could identify and rebuke them), the termination of their exile was also revealed: 70 years in Babylon.

Rashi: 'whose sins were not revealed - their end was not revealed' - the Second Temple generation's sinat chinam was hidden in their hearts. No prophet could point at specific individuals and say 'you hate your neighbor without cause.' Therefore no revealed endpoint for their exile.

Rashi: 'for they put their trust in the Holy One, blessed be He' - the First Temple's wicked leaders (nesi'im) are quoted as saying 'Is not God in our midst? No evil will befall us.' This trust despite their sins is what makes the verse in Micah so biting.

Rashi on 'the First Temple had no sinat chinam?' - the Gemara challenges the distinction between First and Second Temple: wasn't sinat chinam present in the First Temple too? The verse about leaders who eat and drink together yet attack each other with their speech proves it existed.

Rashi: 'that was among the nesi'im of Israel' - the sinat chinam was limited to the princes/leaders (nesi'im). A verse is cited that applies specifically to nesi'im. Therefore the common people could be rebuked and the exile had an end.

Rashi on 'fingernails of the former are better than the belly of the latter': R. Yochanan's statement expressing the greatness of the earlier (First Temple) generations compared to the later ones. Even the least significant part of the earlier generation (fingernails = extremities) surpasses the core (belly) of the later ones.

Rashi: 'on the contrary - the latter are greater': Reish Lakish's counterargument to R. Yochanan. The later generations maintain Torah study despite the additional burden of foreign oppression and exile. This requires more effort and merit.

Rashi: 'the Temple will prove it': R. Yochanan's response to Reish Lakish - the Temple returned to the earlier generations but not to the later ones. This proves the earlier generations had greater merit.
Rashi on 'ask R. Elazar: are the former or latter greater?': the question asked of R. Elazar about relative generational merit, parallel to the R. Yochanan/Reish Lakish dispute.

Rashi: 'the Temple is your witness' - in some versions, R. Elazar agrees with R. Yochanan: the Temple's restoration to earlier generations and its absence from later ones testifies to the former's greater merit.
Rashi on 'Reish Lakish was swimming in the Jordan': a famous aggadic scene. Reish Lakish (R. Shimon ben Lakish) is in the Jordan River; Rabba bar bar Chana comes and extends his hand to help.

Rashi: 'he said to him: by God I hate you Babylonians!' - Reish Lakish's outburst is not personal hatred of Rabba bar bar Chana but historical frustration with Babylonian Jews who did not return to Israel when they had the chance under Ezra.

Rashi: 'if you were like a wall and had come up [to Israel]': quoting Song of Songs 8:9. 'If all of you in Babylonian exile had come up together to Israel in the time of Ezra' - the wall metaphor means unified, solid, all together.

Rashi: 'you would be likened to silver that does not rot' - silver does not decay or corrode under normal conditions. If all Jews had returned with Ezra, the Shechina would have returned fully to the Second Temple, and the temple would have been imperishable like silver.
Rashi: 'since you came up like doors' - doors open and close; they are not solid like walls. Applied to the return: only some came, not all; the return was partial, incomplete.

Rashi: 'you are likened to cedar doors' - cedar wood has a property: sasmagor (a boring rot/worm) hollows it out from within. The Second Temple's divine presence was like cedar: outwardly present but inwardly diminished.

Rashi: 'where the rot controls it' - sasmagor controls parts of the cedar tree, eating it hollow from the interior. Applied to the Second Temple: the Shechina was present but not fully - part of its spiritual power was 'eaten away' by the absence of the Jews who stayed in Babylon.
Rashi on 'what is cedar? Ulla said: sasmagor' - Ulla identifies sasmagor as the name of the rot or boring creature that infests cedar wood. R. Abba's explanation follows.

Rashi: 'it grinds and erodes it from within' - sasmagor specifically attacks the interior of the cedar tree, creating hollow tunnels while the outside appears intact. The metaphor: the Second Temple looked whole but had a diminished spiritual interior.

Rashi: 'why is it called sasmagor? To say: what is the meaning of the cedar comparison?' - R. Abba is asking about the application of the sasmagor metaphor: why were the Jews of the Second Temple era compared to cedar-with-rot rather than to a healthier material?

Rashi on 'R. Abba said: bat kol': after the last three prophets died, prophecy ceased. The bat kol is what remained - a lesser form of divine guidance. The connection to the cedar discussion: even the bat kol is a 'partial' presence, like cedar-with-rot compared to full prophecy.

Rashi: 'a remnant remained for them as what remains of cedar' - just as rotted cedar still has some intact wood remaining, so too Israel still had some connection to divine guidance through the bat kol, even after full prophecy ended.

Rashi: 'what is sasmagor? Why were they likened to cedar in the second Temple context?' - the question is what specifically the cedar metaphor teaches about the spiritual state of the Second Temple period. The bat kol answer: partial divine communication, not none.

Rashi: 'would Reish Lakish speak with Rabba bar bar Chana?' - the Gemara questions: would Reish Lakish, who was a great sage of the Land of Israel, really speak with Rabba bar bar Chana, a Babylonian sage? Reish Lakish was known to avoid associating with those he considered beneath his standards.

Rashi: 'he was the master of the land of Israel and would not speak with one who spoke with Reish Lakish' - Reish Lakish was a dominant figure in the Land of Israel and was notoriously selective about his interlocutors.

Rashi on 'Rav Pappa said: cast a man between them': Rav Pappa resolves the doubt by suggesting an intermediate person. The scene may have involved a chain: either (A) Reish Lakish swimming, Zeira extending hand; or (B) Rabba bar bar Chana swimming, R. Elazar extending hand.

Rashi: 'or Reish Lakish and Zeira' - one possibility: it was Reish Lakish in the water, and Zeira (a Babylonian immigrant to Israel) who extended his hand. Reish Lakish would speak with Zeira since Zeira made aliyah.

Rashi: 'who removes [from a business] without witnesses' - Reish Lakish was careful not to do business with Rabba bar bar Chana without witnesses, implying he would speak to him in formal settings but not trust him in business.
Rashi: 'with Rabba bar bar Chana he would speak' - in this version, it was Rabba bar bar Chana who spoke with Reish Lakish in certain contexts, and the one in the Jordan was R. Elazar.
Rashi on 'when he came before R. Yochanan he said to him' - Reish Lakish came before his teacher R. Yochanan and reported what he had said to the Babylonian sage in the Jordan.

Rashi: 'R. Yochanan said: is that not the case I said to him' - R. Yochanan confirms that the rebuke Reish Lakish gave ('I despise you Babylonians') was the same thing R. Yochanan himself had said to the Babylonian. This validates Reish Lakish's statement.

Rashi: 'not all of them ascended in the days of Ezra' - confirming the historical fact: when Ezra called all Babylonian Jews to return to Israel and rebuild the Temple, only a portion responded. The majority remained in Babylon.

Rashi: 'the Shechina [divine presence] did not dwell in the Second Temple' - the consequence of the partial return. The full restoration of the Shechina that characterized the First Temple was not present in the Second Temple.

Rashi on the verse 'yaft Elokim l'Yafet v'yishkon b'ohalei Shem': this verse from Noah's blessing (Genesis 9:27) is interpreted: 'May God make beautiful for Yafet, and may He dwell in the tents of Shem.' Applied: the Shechina would dwell in the Temple (Shem's descendants) but the Greeks' beauty (Yafet's descendants) would spread. In the Second Temple context: the Greek culture spread but the Shechina's full dwelling did not return.
Rashi: 'aph' (also) at the end of 9b connects to the continuation on 10a. The Shechina's dwelling was conditional on all Israel being present in the Land.

glossary.en: Baseless hatred - hatred without cause. Identified by the Talmud as the sin that caused the destruction of the Second Temple and the longer exile.
Degradation of sacred offerings - a sin of Eli's sons who seized raw meat before the fat was burned on the altar, treating sacred service as an opportunity for personal benefit.
Heavenly voice - the diminished form of divine communication that replaced direct prophecy after the last prophets (Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi) died.
A wood-boring rot or insect that destroys cedar from within. Used metaphorically for the partial Shechina presence in the Second Temple due to incomplete aliyah.
